content.problemStatement:
JJ has a binary string SSS of length NNN. JJ can perform the following operation on SSS:


Select an iii such that 1‚â§i‚â§N1 \le i \le N1‚â§i‚â§N, and flip SiS_iSi‚Äã (i.e. change 000 to 111 and 111 to 000)
JJ wants to minimize the number of inversions in SSS by performing the above operation **at most** KKK times. Can you help JJ do so?

Recall that a pair of indices (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) in SSS is called an inversion if i<ji \lt ji<j and Si>SjS_i \gt S_jSi‚Äã>Sj‚Äã.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and KKK - the length of the binary string SSS and the maximum number of times JJ can perform the given operation.
The second line of each test case contains a binary string SSS of length NNN containing 000s and 111s only.


Output Format
For each test case, output the minimum number of inversions possible after performing the given operation at most KKK times.
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2‚ãÖ1052 \cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: We are allowed to perform at most 333 operations. We can perform the following operation on SSS: 00010‚Äæ111‚Üí000111110001\underline{0}111 \rightarrow 0001111100010‚Äã111‚Üí00011111 which has 000 inversions. Therefore 000 is the answer.
Test case 2: We can perform the following operation on SSS: 1‚Äæ0100‚Üí00100\underline{1}0100 \rightarrow 001001‚Äã0100‚Üí00100 which has 222 inversions. It can be proven that this is the minimum number of inversions possible.
Test case 3: We can perform the following operations on SSS: 11100‚Äæ0‚Üí111010‚Äæ‚Üí1110111110\underline{0}0 \rightarrow 11101\underline{0} \rightarrow 11101111100‚Äã0‚Üí111010‚Äã‚Üí111011 which has 333 inversions. It can be proven that this is the minimum number of inversions possible.
